---
title: "Markdown and Quarto features"
description: "A quick tour of footnotes, math, code blocks, and callouts."
author: "Your Name"
date: "2026-04-15"
categories: [quarto, reference]
---

This post is a small reference for some interesting markdown and Quarto features
beyond the basics like **bold** or *italic* text. You can find on the [Quarto website](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/markdown-basics.html).

## Images

Drop an image into the same folder as the post and reference it with
`![Alt text](filename.jpg)`. If the image is alone on its own line,
Quarto treats the alt text as a **figure caption**:

![A figure with a caption.](figure.png)

## Callouts

::: {.callout-tip}
## Callouts are a Quarto feature
This coloured box is a callout. There are five types: `note`, `tip`,
`warning`, `important`, and `caution`. They're great for highlighting
things without breaking the flow of your writing.
:::

You can learn more about callouts [here](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/callouts.html).

## Footnotes

You can add a footnote anywhere in your text.[^1] Quarto numbers
them and adds a link back to where you cited them.

[^1]: Like this one. Footnote can be added anywhere in the text and are collected at the bottom of the rendered page.

## Math

Quarto understands **LaTeX-style math**, so you can write an equation
inline like $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$, or as a display
block:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

## Code blocks

You can show code in a fenced block. The lowercase `r` tells Quarto to treat it as R code and apply the corresponding syntax highlighting.

```r
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(title = "A plot I am not actually running")
```

To execute the code, use `{r}` with curly braces. Quarto will run the code and insert the output. Running code withing Quarto is another topic and you can find more about it [here for R](https://quarto.org/docs/computations/r.html), [here for Python](https://quarto.org/docs/computations/python.html), and
 [here for Julia](https://quarto.org/docs/computations/julia.html).

## More

For everything else, the [Quarto guide](https://quarto.org/docs/guide/)
is the a nice read.
